MINI MINUTES: RE: Defendant's Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings.

Appearances (in person):
Eduardo Roy, Esq., of Prometheus Partners L.L.P., (415) 527-0255, for Plaintiff.

Amy P. Frenzen, Esq., Deputy San Francisco City Attorney, (415) 554-3885, for Defendant.

The mater is argued, at the conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling. Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for judgment on the pleadings on Plaintiff Eli Georgopoulos's sixth cause of action for "Wrongful Constructive Discharge/Termination of Employment in Violation of Public Policy, the Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing and Implied in Fact Contract" is SUSTAINED without leave to amend. Plaintiff had sufficient notice of the relief that Defendant requests. It is obvious that Defendant is bringing a common law motion for judgment on the pleadings because a statutory motion would be untimely. (See CCP 438(e).) The sixth cause of action actually attempts to plead two causes of action: (1) a Tameny claim (see Tameny v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (1980) 27 Cal.3d 167, 172); and (2) an implied contract claim. Both claims, however, fail as a matter of law against defendant, a public entity. The wrongful termination in violation of public policy or Tameny claim fails. It is a common law claim and Government Code section 815 abolishes all common law forms of liability against a public entity. (McAllister v. Los Angeles Unified School Dist. (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 1198, 1219 ["Miklosy made it clear that a claim for wrongful discharge in violation of public policy may not be brought against a public entity."].) The implied contract claim fails because the terms of public employment are fixed by statute. (Miller v. California (1977) 18 Cal.3d 808, 813 ["[I]t is well settled in California that public employment is not held by contract but by statute"]; Shoemaker v. Myers (1990) 52 Cal.3d 1, 23-24 [same].)

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn. Clerk: Robert Woods. Court Reporter: Ryan Wheeler; CSR: 13717; T: 760.805.3974. (302/JMQ)

***After hearing, an order is signed.

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-25-2023 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. EDUARDO G. ROY, Esq. of Prometheus Partners L.L.P. (Tel: 415-527-0255) for plaintiff;
Atty. JANICE P. BROWN, Esq. of Meyers Nave (Tel: 619-330-1700) for defendant City and County of San Francisco.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's (City and County of San Francisco) demurrer to the fifth cause of action of the first amended complaint (FAC) is sustained without leave to amend.

The demurrer challenges only the FAC's fifth cause of action (retaliation for opposing illegal activities in violation of Labor Code 1102.5) and on only one ground: alleged failure to file a government claim within the required six-month period.

"The Government Claims Act (section 810 et seq.) establishes certain conditions precedent to the filing of a lawsuit against a public entity. As relevant here, a plaintiff must timely file a claim for money or damages with the public entity. The failure to do so bars the plaintiff from bringing suit against that entity." (Le Mere v. Los Angeles Unified Sch. Dist. (2020) 35 Cal.App.5th 237, 246.) Plaintiff filed a government claim after filing his original complaint, but before filing the FAC. Plaintiff then filed his FAC in July 2022.

Plaintiff argues that the government claim was timely filed because it included conduct occurring after the lawsuit was filed. Not so. "Nothing in the law suggests, however, that a public entity's alleged misconduct serves to lift an existing procedural bar or relieve a plaintiff from following the legislatively prescribed claim procedures." (Id. at 247.) Pre-lawsuit compliance with the claim filing requirement is a condition precedent which has not been met. (See id. ["Appellant was required to file a claim with LAUSD before filing her lawsuit. She failed to do so. The lawsuit is therefore barred."])

Plaintiff further argues that the claim filing requirement was satisfied because the government claim was filed before the FAC which supersedes the prior complaint. The Court in Le Mere notes that this runs contrary to the purpose of the Government Claims Act: "At that point, it is too late to provide the public entity sufficient information to enable it to adequately investigate claims and to settle them, if appropriate, without the expense of litigation." (Id.)

Finally, Plaintiff argues that Le Mere is distinguishable because it did not involve an amended pleading that alleges post-filing conduct. Not so. In Le Mere, the plaintiff (Le Mere) filed her original complaint on February 10, 2015 and her FAC on September 30, 2015. On February 16, 2016, Le Mere filed a government tort claim "relating to her constructive discharge on August 17, 2015." (Le Mere, Appellant's Brief at 15.) This constructive discharge in August 2015 took place after the original complaint filed on February 10, 2015, and before the SAC filed in April 2016. (Le Mere, 35 Cal.App.5th at 241.) On appeal, Le Mere argued that the requirement of a pre-lawsuit government tort claim is problematic when the harassment does not occur until after the complaint is filed. (Id. at 246.) The Court of Appeal rejected this argument, reasoning that since Le Mere named the defendant in the original complaint alleging retaliation, she was required to file a government claim before filing the lawsuit. (Id. at 246-47.) Here, Plaintiff named the Defendant in his original complaint alleging retaliation under Gov. Code section 1102.5, and his failure to file a pre-lawsuit government claim bars this cause of action.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Brianna Rudd, CSR No. 13668, Email: briannarudd@gmail.com, Tel: 209-747-1306 (302/RBU)
